metadata.title: Build a Hypothesis-Testing Drill with Claude Code
metadata.color_semantics: teardown: INK #2A1A0E neutral/normal; CRIMSON #C8102E key finding/vulnerability; SLATE #545454 structure/neutral

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

Good case analysis is hypothesis-driven, not data-gathering-driven. Most practice sessions give you data without forcing you to form a hypothesis first. The discipline is articulating what you expect before you look at what is there. What does a hypothesis-testing loop actually look like when you can run it in sixty seconds?
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- title card: HYPOTHESIS-TESTING DRILL; the problem: data-gathering vs. hypothesis-first discipline highlighted

beats.narration_text: In the terminal: ask Claude to build a Python CLI that takes a case setup and one data point, generates three testable hypotheses, for each asks what data would confirm or disconfirm it, then lets you request one more data point and shows how the hypothesis space narrows.

props.command:
claude "Build a Python CLI hypothesis-testing drill.
Input: a case setup string and one data point.
Output: (1) three testable hypotheses consistent
with the data, (2) for each, what data would
confirm or disconfirm it.
Then prompt the user to request one additional
data point from a hidden set defined in the script,
and show how the hypothesis space narrows.
Use the anthropic Python SDK."

beats.narration_text: Read the code before trusting it. The script structures three passes through Claude: first generating the hypotheses from the case setup and data point, then for each hypothesis asking what would confirm or disconfirm it, then re-running after the new data point is selected. Verify the hypothesis generation prompt is structured to produce genuinely distinct propositions, not paraphrases.

props.code:
# hypothesis_drill.py -- Claude Code output
import anthropic

HIDDEN_DATA = {
    "1": "Revenue per unit fell 12% YoY",
    "2": "Variable costs rose 3% YoY",
    "3": "Market volume grew 8% YoY",
}

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def hypothesize(setup, datapoint):
    msg = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5", max_tokens=1024,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content":
            f"Case: {setup}\nData: {datapoint}\n"
            "Generate 3 falsifiable hypotheses. "
            "For each: what data confirms/disconfirms?"}]
    )
    return msg.content[0].text

setup = "Retailer margins fell 5pp in 12 months"
dp1 = "Customer count is flat"
print(hypothesize(setup, dp1))
choice = input("Request data point (1/2/3): ")
print(hypothesize(setup, HIDDEN_DATA[choice]))

beats.narration_text: Run it. The drill opens on a retailer case: margins fell five points, customer count is flat. Three hypotheses appear — revenue per unit is the driver, cost structure has shifted, or mix has changed toward lower-margin categories. For each, the confirmation and disconfirmation data are explicit. The user selects data point one: revenue per unit fell twelve percent.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- screen-recording of terminal: three hypotheses with confirm/disconfirm data; data point selection prompt

beats.narration_text: Add the comparison mode. The chapter says every number needs a reference point. After the first iteration, the script should automatically ask Claude what comparison would make the most recently requested data point actually informative.

props.command:
claude "Modify hypothesis_drill.py:
After the user picks a data point, automatically
ask Claude: 'What comparison would make this
data point actually informative?' and display
the generated comparison question below the
narrowed hypothesis set."

beats.narration_text: The revised output. Hypothesis two is eliminated — costs did not move, so the margin compression is not on the cost side. The comparison question appears below: revenue per unit fell twelve percent — but what did competitors' revenue per unit do over the same period? Without that reference, twelve percent is not yet a fact, it is a magnitude without meaning.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- screen-recording: narrowed hypothesis set (one eliminated) plus comparison question below

beats.narration_text: Hypothesis-testing feels slower than data gathering in the moment but produces better recommendations at the end. The discipline is forming the testable proposition before you look at the data. This loop makes that discipline visible by forcing you to articulate what you expect before you see what is there.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- summary card: hypothesis-first vs. data-gathering discipline; the loop's value stated

beats.narration_text: Supply three to five real case setups with hidden data tables. The drill works on synthetic cases but is shallower without a real dataset to narrow against. The comparison question is the one to watch: if Claude cannot generate a meaningful reference point, the data point you chose is not yet informative.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- next steps card: supply real case setups; watch the comparison question quality; use the elimination step

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI. Then take it apart. At Nik Bear Brown, nikbearbrown dot com.
